items.passage:
Alloys are metallic materials made by combining a base metal with one or more other elements to improve properties such as strength, hardness, or resistance to corrosion. The added elements disrupt the orderly arrangement of atoms in the base metal, making it harder for the metal's internal layers to slide past one another. This atomic interference, known as solid-solution strengthening, is the foundation of nearly all engineering metals in use today.

This strengthening mechanism becomes especially visible in steel, an alloy of iron and a small amount of carbon. [■] The carbon atoms lodge in the gaps between iron atoms and obstruct the movement of defects that would otherwise let the metal deform easily. [■] By carefully adjusting the carbon content, metallurgists can produce steels that range from soft and bendable to extremely hard. [■]

Beyond steel, the same principle guides the design of high-performance alloys for demanding settings. Aluminum alloyed with copper, for instance, becomes light yet strong enough for aircraft frames. [■] Despite their versatility, these materials face limits: adding too much of an element can make an alloy brittle, so each composition reflects a careful balance that engineers may struggle to optimize.
paragraphs: Alloys are metallic materials made by combining a base metal with one or more other elements to improve properties such as strength, hardness, or resistance to corrosion. The added elements disrupt the orderly arrangement of atoms in the base metal, making it harder for the metal's internal layers to slide past one another. This atomic interference, known as solid-solution strengthening, is the foundation of nearly all engineering metals in use today.

This strengthening mechanism becomes especially visible in steel, an alloy of iron and a small amount of carbon. The carbon atoms lodge in the gaps between iron atoms and obstruct the movement of defects that would otherwise let the metal deform easily. By carefully adjusting the carbon content, metallurgists can produce steels that range from soft and bendable to extremely hard.

Beyond steel, the same principle guides the design of high-performance alloys for demanding settings. Aluminum alloyed with copper, for instance, becomes light yet strong enough for aircraft frames. Despite their versatility, these materials face limits: adding too much of an element can make an alloy brittle, so each composition reflects a careful balance that engineers may struggle to optimize.

questions.stem: The word 'obstruct' in paragraph 2 is closest in meaning to

options.A: accelerate
options.B: measure
options.C: reverse
options.D: block

questions.explanation: The passage says carbon atoms 'obstruct the movement of defects that would otherwise let the metal deform easily,' so the word means to block or impede that movement.

questions.stem: According to paragraph 1, what do added elements do to the base metal?

options.A: They disrupt the orderly arrangement of its atoms.
options.B: They dissolve the metal into a liquid form.
options.C: They remove impurities left from smelting.
options.D: They lower the metal's melting temperature sharply.

questions.explanation: Paragraph 1 states that 'the added elements disrupt the orderly arrangement of atoms in the base metal,' which option A paraphrases.

questions.stem: What can be inferred from paragraph 2 about steels with higher carbon content?

options.A: They contain no iron atoms at all.
options.B: They are easier to bend than pure iron.
options.C: They tend to be harder and less easily deformed.
options.D: They lose their metallic character entirely.

questions.explanation: Since carbon obstructs the defects that let metal deform and adjusting carbon yields steels 'from soft and bendable to extremely hard,' more carbon implies harder, less deformable steel.

questions.stem: Why does the author mention aircraft frames in paragraph 3?

options.A: To illustrate how alloying serves demanding real-world applications
options.B: To argue that aluminum is superior to all other metals
options.C: To explain why steel is unsuitable for any structure
options.D: To describe the historical origins of metallurgy

questions.explanation: Aircraft frames are given as an example of aluminum-copper alloys being 'light yet strong enough,' illustrating that the strengthening principle serves demanding applications.

questions.stem: There are four locations [■] in the passage that indicate where the following sentence could be added. 'And because this obstruction grows stronger as more carbon is added, even small changes in carbon content noticeably alter the metal's behavior.' Where would the sentence best fit? Select a location to add the sentence to the passage.

options.A: Slot 1
options.B: Slot 2
options.C: Slot 3
options.D: Slot 4

questions.explanation: The sentence refers back to the 'obstruction' described just before slot 2 and sets up the next sentence about adjusting carbon content, so slot 2 fits best.

items.passage:
Attention is the cognitive process by which the mind selects certain stimuli for focused processing while filtering others out. Early researchers in the 1950s pictured attention as a physical bottleneck: a narrow channel through which only one stream of information could pass at a time. [■] In this view, unattended messages were blocked almost immediately, before their meaning could be registered by the mind.

This early model soon encountered evidence it could not explain. Listeners ignoring one conversation still noticed their own name spoken in it, suggesting that unattended input was analyzed more deeply than a strict filter allowed. [■] Researchers therefore revised the theory, proposing that unattended signals were not blocked outright but merely weakened, or attenuated, leaving important words able to break through. [■]

This attenuation account reshaped how psychologists understand selection. Attention came to be seen as a flexible system that allocates limited resources rather than a rigid gate. [■] Modern studies using brain imaging support this graded picture, tracing how focus strengthens some neural signals while dampening others. However, the precise stage at which selection occurs remains contested, and no single model yet accounts for every finding.
paragraphs: Attention is the cognitive process by which the mind selects certain stimuli for focused processing while filtering others out. Early researchers in the 1950s pictured attention as a physical bottleneck: a narrow channel through which only one stream of information could pass at a time. In this view, unattended messages were blocked almost immediately, before their meaning could be registered by the mind.

This early model soon encountered evidence it could not explain. Listeners ignoring one conversation still noticed their own name spoken in it, suggesting that unattended input was analyzed more deeply than a strict filter allowed. Researchers therefore revised the theory, proposing that unattended signals were not blocked outright but merely weakened, or attenuated, leaving important words able to break through.

This attenuation account reshaped how psychologists understand selection. Attention came to be seen as a flexible system that allocates limited resources rather than a rigid gate. Modern studies using brain imaging support this graded picture, tracing how focus strengthens some neural signals while dampening others. However, the precise stage at which selection occurs remains contested, and no single model yet accounts for every finding.

questions.stem: The word 'attenuated' in paragraph 2 is closest in meaning to

options.A: amplified
options.B: weakened
options.C: recorded
options.D: repeated

questions.explanation: The passage equates 'attenuated' with signals that are 'not blocked outright but merely weakened,' so the word means weakened.

questions.stem: According to paragraph 1, how did early researchers in the 1950s picture attention?

options.A: As a physical bottleneck allowing one stream at a time
options.B: As a flexible system sharing resources across streams
options.C: As a process driven mainly by brain imaging data
options.D: As a graded filter that weakened incoming signals

questions.explanation: Paragraph 1 says early researchers 'pictured attention as a physical bottleneck: a narrow channel through which only one stream of information could pass at a time.'

questions.stem: What does 'it' refer to in the phrase 'evidence it could not explain' in paragraph 2?

options.A: the mind
options.B: the listener
options.C: the conversation
options.D: the early model

questions.explanation: The sentence 'This early model soon encountered evidence it could not explain' uses 'it' to refer back to 'this early model,' the bottleneck theory.

questions.stem: What can be inferred from paragraph 2 about hearing one's own name in an ignored conversation?

options.A: Unattended input receives more processing than a strict filter permits.
options.B: Names are stored in a separate memory unrelated to attention.
options.C: Listeners cannot detect any unattended speech whatsoever.
options.D: The bottleneck model fully predicted this observation.

questions.explanation: The example is said to suggest 'that unattended input was analyzed more deeply than a strict filter allowed,' implying more processing than a strict filter permits.

questions.stem: There are four locations [■] in the passage that indicate where the following sentence could be added. 'For example, words that carried personal meaning could still capture a listener's awareness despite being ignored.' Where would the sentence best fit? Select a location to add the sentence to the passage.

options.A: Slot 1
options.B: Slot 2
options.C: Slot 3
options.D: Slot 4

questions.explanation: The sentence elaborates the preceding point that listeners noticed their own name, illustrating words with personal meaning breaking through, so slot 3 fits best.

items.passage:
Bipedalism, the habit of walking upright on two legs, is a defining trait that distinguishes early human ancestors from other apes. Unlike the occasional two-legged movement of modern chimpanzees, bipedalism in the human lineage became the primary form of travel, reshaping the skeleton from the spine down to the foot. Fossils show this posture emerging in Africa several million years ago, long before brains began to enlarge.

This upright stance works through a series of coordinated skeletal changes. The pelvis broadened and shortened to support the trunk, while the thigh bones angled inward to place the feet beneath the body's center of gravity. The spine developed an S-shaped curve that balances the head over the hips, allowing a person to stand and walk for long periods without tiring quickly.

These anatomical shifts carried significance far beyond locomotion. Freed hands could carry food, tools, or infants across distances, and an upright posture may have helped early humans regulate body heat under the open sun. However, walking on two legs also narrowed the birth canal, creating difficulties in childbirth that continue to challenge explanations of how the trait first spread.
paragraphs: Bipedalism, the habit of walking upright on two legs, is a defining trait that distinguishes early human ancestors from other apes. Unlike the occasional two-legged movement of modern chimpanzees, bipedalism in the human lineage became the primary form of travel, reshaping the skeleton from the spine down to the foot. Fossils show this posture emerging in Africa several million years ago, long before brains began to enlarge.

This upright stance works through a series of coordinated skeletal changes. The pelvis broadened and shortened to support the trunk, while the thigh bones angled inward to place the feet beneath the body's center of gravity. The spine developed an S-shaped curve that balances the head over the hips, allowing a person to stand and walk for long periods without tiring quickly.

These anatomical shifts carried significance far beyond locomotion. Freed hands could carry food, tools, or infants across distances, and an upright posture may have helped early humans regulate body heat under the open sun. However, walking on two legs also narrowed the birth canal, creating difficulties in childbirth that continue to challenge explanations of how the trait first spread.

questions.stem: The word 'distinguishes' in paragraph 1 is closest in meaning to

options.A: connects
options.B: separates
options.C: weakens
options.D: delays

questions.explanation: Bipedalism is a trait that 'distinguishes early human ancestors from other apes,' meaning it sets them apart or separates them.

questions.stem: According to paragraph 2, what change occurred in the pelvis?

options.A: It broadened and shortened to support the trunk.
options.B: It narrowed and lengthened to extend the legs.
options.C: It fused with the spine to limit movement.
options.D: It rotated backward to lower the center of gravity.

questions.explanation: Paragraph 2 states 'The pelvis broadened and shortened to support the trunk,' which option A restates.

questions.stem: What can be inferred from paragraph 1 about the relationship between bipedalism and brain size?

options.A: Larger brains developed before upright walking appeared.
options.B: Upright walking appeared before brains began to enlarge.
options.C: Brain size and bipedalism evolved at exactly the same time.
options.D: Bipedalism prevented the human brain from growing.

questions.explanation: The passage notes the upright posture emerged 'long before brains began to enlarge,' implying bipedalism preceded brain expansion.

questions.stem: Why does the author mention carrying food, tools, or infants in paragraph 3?

options.A: To question whether early humans used tools at all
options.B: To explain how the birth canal became narrower
options.C: To illustrate benefits that freed hands made possible
options.D: To describe the structure of the human spine

questions.explanation: The examples follow the statement that 'Freed hands could carry food, tools, or infants,' illustrating the advantages that an upright posture made possible.

questions.stem: According to the passage, all of the following are described as features or consequences of bipedalism EXCEPT

options.A: an S-shaped curve in the spine
options.B: a broadened, shortened pelvis
options.C: hands freed to carry objects
options.D: a widened birth canal easing childbirth

questions.explanation: The passage says bipedalism 'narrowed the birth canal, creating difficulties in childbirth,' the opposite of a widened canal easing childbirth, while A, B, and C are all stated.

items.passage:
A composite is a material made by combining two distinct components: a matrix that holds everything together and a reinforcement that provides strength. The two parts remain physically separate within the finished material, yet they act as a single unit. This arrangement lets engineers join the useful qualities of different substances, producing a material that performs better than either component alone.

The combination works because each component handles a different job. The reinforcement, often stiff fibers of glass or carbon, carries most of the load placed on the material, while the surrounding matrix, usually a plastic resin, transfers forces between the fibers and protects them from damage. Because the fibers can be aligned in chosen directions, the material can be made especially strong exactly where strength is needed.

This design principle has reshaped many modern industries. Carbon-fiber composites make aircraft and bicycles light yet stiff, and glass-fiber composites form the hulls of boats and the blades of wind turbines. However, composites can be costly to manufacture and difficult to recycle once they are damaged, so engineers continue to search for cheaper and more sustainable alternatives.
paragraphs: A composite is a material made by combining two distinct components: a matrix that holds everything together and a reinforcement that provides strength. The two parts remain physically separate within the finished material, yet they act as a single unit. This arrangement lets engineers join the useful qualities of different substances, producing a material that performs better than either component alone.

The combination works because each component handles a different job. The reinforcement, often stiff fibers of glass or carbon, carries most of the load placed on the material, while the surrounding matrix, usually a plastic resin, transfers forces between the fibers and protects them from damage. Because the fibers can be aligned in chosen directions, the material can be made especially strong exactly where strength is needed.

This design principle has reshaped many modern industries. Carbon-fiber composites make aircraft and bicycles light yet stiff, and glass-fiber composites form the hulls of boats and the blades of wind turbines. However, composites can be costly to manufacture and difficult to recycle once they are damaged, so engineers continue to search for cheaper and more sustainable alternatives.

questions.stem: What is the passage mainly about?

options.A: The history of glass and carbon fiber manufacturing
options.B: The reasons aircraft must be both light and stiff
options.C: The environmental costs of recycling damaged materials
options.D: How composites combine two components for better performance

questions.explanation: The passage defines composites, explains how the matrix and reinforcement work together, and surveys their applications, so it is mainly about how composites combine components for better performance.

questions.stem: According to paragraph 2, what is the main role of the reinforcement?

options.A: To transfer forces between separate fibers
options.B: To carry most of the load placed on the material
options.C: To protect the matrix from physical damage
options.D: To lower the cost of manufacturing the material

questions.explanation: Paragraph 2 states the reinforcement 'carries most of the load placed on the material,' which option B restates.

questions.stem: How does paragraph 2 relate to paragraph 1?

options.A: It contradicts the definition given in paragraph 1.
options.B: It lists industries unrelated to paragraph 1.
options.C: It explains the mechanism behind the idea defined in paragraph 1.
options.D: It questions whether composites truly exist.

questions.explanation: Paragraph 1 defines a composite, and paragraph 2 explains how its components 'handle a different job,' detailing the mechanism behind that definition.

questions.stem: What can be inferred from paragraph 2 about aligning the fibers in chosen directions?

options.A: It makes the matrix unnecessary for the material.
options.B: It lets engineers concentrate strength where it is required.
options.C: It weakens the material in every direction equally.
options.D: It prevents the reinforcement from carrying any load.

questions.explanation: The passage says fibers 'can be aligned in chosen directions' so the material is 'especially strong exactly where strength is needed,' implying strength can be concentrated where required.

questions.stem: According to paragraph 3, what is one drawback of composites?

options.A: They can be costly to manufacture and hard to recycle.
options.B: They are too heavy for use in aircraft.
options.C: They cannot be shaped into boat hulls.
options.D: They lose all strength when fibers are aligned.

questions.explanation: Paragraph 3 notes that 'composites can be costly to manufacture and difficult to recycle once they are damaged,' matching option A.
